Maximize |(A+B) ∩ (A∪B)| when A,B partition {1..2n} inside {1..3n}

Let n=100 and U={1,2,...,300}. Choose disjoint sets A,B ⊂ U with |A|=|B|=100 and with the additional constraint
  A ∪ B = {1,2,...,200}.
(So the remaining labels 201,...,300 are "unused".)

Define the sumset
  E = A + B = {a+b : a\inA, b\inB}.

Determine the maximum possible value of
  |E ∩ (A ∪ B)|.

Solution.
Step 1 (sumset_construction; derive_basic_obstructions).
Since A ∪ B = {1,2,...,200}, we are maximizing
  |(A+B) ∩ {1,2,...,200}|.
Note that 1 ∉ A+B always (smallest possible sum is 1+2=3).
Also 2 ∉ A+B always: the only way to write 2 as a sum of positive integers is 1+1, but 1 cannot lie in both A and B (they are disjoint).
Hence at least two elements of {1,...,200} are missing from (A+B), so a trivial ceiling is
  |(A+B) ∩ {1,...,200}| \leq 200 - 2 = 198.
We will prove a stronger universal ceiling 197 and then exhibit a construction achieving 197.

Step 2 (forcing_chain_induction; pigeonhole_size_contradiction).
We prove that for any such partition A,B of {1,...,200}, the set (A+B) cannot contain all integers 3,4,...,200.
Equivalently, besides 1 and 2, at least one more number in {3,...,200} must also be missing, so
  |(A+B) ∩ {1,...,200}| \leq 200 - 3 = 197.

Assume for contradiction that
  {3,4,...,200} ⊆ A+B.                                               (★)

Swap names of A and B if needed so that 1 \in A.

Claim: 2 ∉ A is forced under (★).
If 2 \in A, then 3 cannot be in A+B because the only representation of 3 as a sum of two positive integers is 1+2, which would be monochromatic (both in A), contradicting (★).
Therefore 2 \in B.

Now we show that (★) forces B to contain far too many small integers.

First, because 4 \in A+B and 4 can only be written as 1+3 or 2+2, and 2+2 is impossible (needs two copies of 2),
we must have 3 \in B (so that 1\inA and 3\inB give 1+3=4).

Next, because 5 \in A+B and 5 = 1+4 = 2+3:
- 2 and 3 are both in B, so 2+3 is monochromatic and cannot witness 5 \in A+B,
so we must have 4 \in B to make 1+4 bichromatic.

Similarly, because 6 \in A+B and 6 = 1+5 = 2+4 = 3+3:
- 2 and 4 are in B, so 2+4 is monochromatic,
- 3+3 is impossible (same element twice),
so we must have 5 \in B to make 1+5 bichromatic.

This "forcing" continues:

Inductive claim:
If {2,3,...,k} ⊆ B for some k with 3 \leq k \leq 199, and if (k+2) \in A+B, then (k+1) \in B.

Proof:
Any representation of k+2 as a+b with a\inA, b\inB must use a\neqb and a+b=k+2.
Consider all pairs (i, k+2-i) with i=2,3,...,k.
Both i and k+2-i lie in {2,...,k}, hence both lie in B by hypothesis, so every such pair is monochromatic and cannot represent k+2 as a cross-sum.
Thus the only possible bichromatic representation is 1+(k+1), and since 1\inA, we must have k+1 \in B.

Applying the induction starting from 2,3 \in B and using (★) (which asserts every k+2 from 5 up to 200 is in A+B),
we conclude
  {2,3,4,...,199} ⊆ B.
But that set has 198 elements, contradicting |B|=100.

Therefore (★) is impossible: at least one integer in {3,...,200} is missing from A+B.
Together with 1 and 2 missing, we obtain the universal upper bound
  |(A+B) ∩ {1,...,200}| \leq 197.

Step 3 (explicit_extremal_construction; representation_check_for_interval).
We now exhibit A,B achieving 197.

Let
  A = {1} ∪ {2,4,6,...,198}   (i.e., 1 and all even numbers from 2 to 198),
  B = {3,5,7,...,199} ∪ {200} (i.e., all odd numbers from 3 to 199, plus 200).
Then A and B are disjoint, |A|=|B|=100, and A∪B={1,2,...,200} as required.

We claim:
  (A+B) ∩ {1,...,200} = {4,5,6,...,200},
so the intersection size is 200-4+1 = 197.

Proof of coverage:
- For any even m with 4 \leq m \leq 200:
  m = 1 + (m-1),
and m-1 is odd, at least 3 and at most 199, hence (m-1) \in B. Since 1 \in A, we get m \in A+B.
- For any odd m with 5 \leq m \leq 199:
  m = 2 + (m-2),
and m-2 is odd, at least 3 and at most 197, hence (m-2) \in B. Since 2 \in A, we get m \in A+B.
- The remaining endpoint m=200 is already covered by the even case: 200 = 1 + 199 with 199 \in B.

Non-coverage of 1,2,3:
- 1 and 2 are impossible sums of positive integers from disjoint sets as in Step 1.
- 3 would require 1+2 with 1\inA and 2\inA (not in B), so 3 ∉ A+B.

Thus exactly {1,2,3} are missing and all of {4,...,200} are present, so
  |(A+B) ∩ (A∪B)| = 197.

Step 4 (conclude_optimal_and_specialize_n_100).
Step 2 gives an upper bound 197 for n=100, and Step 3 achieves 197, so the maximum is 197.

Answer: 197.

3) Uniqueness Test
- Upper bound certificate: Steps 1-2 prove that for any admissible A,B with A∪B={1,...,200}, at least three labels in {1,...,200} are not in A+B: always 1 and 2, and at least one additional label in {3,...,200} by the forcing-chain size contradiction. Hence |(A+B)∩(A∪B)| \leq 197.
- Attainment certificate: Step 3 gives explicit sets A,B meeting all constraints and satisfying (A+B)∩(A∪B) = {4,...,200}, so |(A+B)∩(A∪B)| = 197.
- Since a maximum is both (i) bounded above by 197 for all choices and (ii) achieved by at least one choice, the maximum value is uniquely determined and equals 197.

197
